title: Key-Value Store (Generic)
description: Key-value store for sub-millisecond point lookups on simple data structures: strings, counters, hashes, sorted sets. Suited to caching, session storage, rate limiting, and real-time leaderboards. Common implementations include Redis, Memcached, and DynamoDB in key-value mode.

provides.reasoning: This is the fundamental defining characteristic of key-value store patterns, which store and retrieve data using simple key-value pairs as their primary data model. All implementations mentioned (Redis, Memcached, DynamoDB in KV mode) are designed specifically to provide this capability.

Key-value stores are optimized for extremely fast read operations through in-memory storage or highly optimized access patterns, typically achieving sub-millisecond response times. This low latency is essential for use cases like caching, session storage, and rate limiting.

Key-value stores excel at both read and write operations, typically achieving sub-millisecond write latencies. This is essential for use cases like rate limiting and real-time leaderboards mentioned in the description.
Caching is explicitly mentioned as a primary use case in the pattern description. Key-value stores are commonly used as caching layers due to their fast access patterns and simple data model.

Many key-value store implementations (Redis, Memcached) operate primarily in-memory for maximum performance. This is a common characteristic that enables the sub-millisecond latencies these systems provide.

Session storage is explicitly mentioned as a primary use case in the pattern description. Key-value stores are well-suited for this due to their fast lookups and ability to store user session data efficiently.

Key-value stores are a primary mechanism for managing application state — including session state, feature flags, counters, and distributed locks — making state-management a natural output of this pattern.

requires.reasoning: A key-value store needs computational resources to run the database engine, handle requests, and manage data structures in memory or on disk.
Key-value stores need network connectivity to accept client connections and serve requests. This is essential for any distributed or networked key-value store implementation.

eviction_policy.description: Eviction policy when memory is full. lru=least recently used (best for caching), lfu=least frequently used (best for popular items), ttl=expire by time, no-eviction=return errors when full

persistence.description: Enable data persistence to disk. True=durable storage with performance overhead, False=in-memory only with maximum speed but data loss on restart

max_memory_gb.description: Maximum memory allocation in GB. Higher values support more data but increase costs

replication.description: Enable data replication across multiple nodes for high availability. Increases reliability but adds complexity and cost

ttl_default_seconds.description: Default time-to-live in seconds for keys (0=no expiration, max=30 days). Automatic expiration useful for caching and session management

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Generic key-value store costs vary widely by implementation. Min=$0 represents free tiers (Redis Cloud 30MB free, AWS DynamoDB 25GB free tier, Upstash serverless free tier). Max=$300 represents small-to-medium production scenarios: Redis managed service (cache.t3.small ~$50-80/month), DynamoDB with moderate throughput (5-10 WCU/RCU ~$50-100/month), or Memcached cluster (2x cache.t3.micro ~$40/month) plus data transfer and storage. Enterprise production clusters with high availability, multi-region replication, and significant throughput would exceed this range ($1000-5000+/month), but this pattern represents generic/starter implementations.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-low complexity implementation. Key-value stores have simple data models (put/get/delete operations), making them easier to adopt than relational databases. However, adoption costs include: choosing the right implementation (Redis vs DynamoDB vs Memcached - 4-8 hours research), understanding TTL and eviction policies (4-8 hours), implementing connection pooling and error handling (8-16 hours), data serialization strategy (4-8 hours), and basic monitoring setup (4-8 hours). Total ~24-48 hours at $100-150/hour blended rate = $2,400-7,200, but with AI coding assistants reducing implementation time by 70-80%, realistic cost is $500-1,000. Setting at $500 assuming team has basic distributed systems knowledge.
provenance.licenseCost: Generic key-value pattern itself has no licensing cost. Common implementations are open-source: Redis (BSD-3-Clause), Memcached (BSD), and cloud-managed versions (AWS DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB, GCP Firestore) use pay-as-you-go pricing without separate license fees. Commercial Redis Enterprise exists but isn't required for this generic pattern. Enterprise support contracts optional but not included in base license cost.

supports_nfr.reason: Key-value stores like Redis Cloud achieve up to 99.99% availability.
Key-value stores provide sub-millisecond to low single-digit millisecond p95 latency.
P99 latency floor accounts for network overhead and occasional cache misses or replication lag.
Automatic failover in replicated key-value stores achieves sub-minute RTO with health checks.
Key-value stores handle up to 100K operations per second per node, scaling to millions per hour.
Key-value stores provide extremely high read throughput with sub-millisecond latency.
Key-value stores provide extremely high write throughput with optimized single-key operations.
Key-value stores support multiple tenant isolation strategies via key prefixing, namespaces, or separate instances.
Key-value stores can store any data type including PII depending on application requirements.
GDPR compliance is achievable with proper key-value store configuration for encryption and data deletion.
HIPAA compliance is achievable with encryption at rest, in transit, and audit logging enabled.
CCPA compliance is achievable with data access controls and deletion capabilities for consumer data.
SOX compliance is achievable with audit trails, access controls, and backup retention policies.
Single-node or synchronous replication modes guarantee read-your-writes, while async replication may not.
Key-value stores support both durable persistence modes and pure in-memory volatile configurations.

supports_constraints.reason: This pattern specifically provides key-value store functionality as its core capability.

requires_constraints.reason: Generic key-value pattern is cloud-agnostic and deployable across all cloud providers and on-premises.
Key-value stores are infrastructure components accessible via client libraries in all major languages.
Key-value stores are backend data infrastructure consumable by any platform type.
Key-value stores are frequently used for caching but also support persistent storage use cases.
Key-value stores can support async messaging patterns but are not limited to them.
Key-value stores can cache AI inference results or feature vectors but are not AI-specific.
Key-value stores support both single-tenant and multi-tenant architectures via key namespacing.
Key-value stores can support batch processing workloads but are optimized for real-time access.
Some key-value stores support transactions while others are optimized for single-key atomicity.
Key-value stores can support real-time streaming patterns via pub/sub or as state stores.
Some key-value stores support vector search extensions but it is not a core capability.
Key-value stores can store JSON documents as values but are not specialized document databases.
Key-value stores do not provide native graph capabilities but can store graph-like data structures.
Key-value stores can store time-series data but lack specialized time-series query optimizations.
Key-value stores support OLTP workloads with high-speed transactional operations on single keys.
Key-value stores are not optimized for OLAP but can serve as caching layers for analytical queries.

requires_nfr.reason: Key-value stores like Redis Cloud achieve up to 99.99% availability.
Key-value stores provide sub-millisecond to low single-digit millisecond p95 latency.
P99 latency floor accounts for network overhead and occasional cache misses or replication lag.
Automatic failover in replicated key-value stores achieves sub-minute RTO with health checks.
Synchronous replication provides near-zero RPO, while async replication allows any RPO target.
Key-value stores handle up to 100K operations per second per node, scaling to millions per hour.
Key-value stores provide extremely high read throughput with sub-millisecond latency.
Key-value stores provide extremely high write throughput with optimized single-key operations.
